items.question: Is this HEIC converter safe to use?
items.answer: Yes, absolutely. All file conversion happens directly in your browser using JavaScript. Your files are never uploaded to any server — your photos remain 100% private on your device.

items.question: What is a HEIC file?
items.answer: HEIC (High Efficiency Image Container) is the default photo format on Apple iPhones and iPads since iOS 11. It stores photos at roughly half the size of JPEG at the same quality, but many devices and apps can't open it — which is why converting to JPG or PNG is so useful.

items.question: How do I open HEIC files on Windows or Android?
items.answer: Windows and most Android devices can't open HEIC files natively. The fastest fix is to convert the HEIC file to JPG right here — no software to install. On Windows 10/11 you can alternatively install the HEIF Image Extensions from the Microsoft Store.

items.question: Why are my iPhone photos saved as HEIC?
items.answer: Since iOS 11, iPhones save photos as HEIC by default to save storage space. If you prefer JPEG, go to Settings > Camera > Formats and choose 'Most Compatible' — or simply convert your existing HEIC photos here whenever you need them.

items.question: Is there a file size limit?
items.answer: There is no strict file size limit since all processing happens in your browser. Very large files (over 50MB) may just take a little longer to convert depending on your device.

items.question: Do I need to create an account?
items.answer: No. OpenMyHEIC is completely free with no registration, no account, and no limit on the number of conversions.

jpg.question: How do I convert HEIC to JPG?
jpg.answer: Drag and drop your HEIC files into the converter above (or click to browse), adjust the quality if you like, and press Convert. Your JPG downloads instantly — the whole conversion happens in your browser.

jpg.question: How can I change HEIC to JPG without installing software?
jpg.answer: That's exactly what this page does. There's nothing to install and nothing to upload: the HEIC file is converted to JPG locally in your browser, on Windows, Mac, Linux, Android or iPhone.

jpg.question: Does converting HEIC to JPG lose quality?
jpg.answer: JPG is a compressed format, but at the default 92% quality setting the difference is invisible to the eye. You can raise the quality slider to 100% for maximum fidelity or lower it for smaller files.

jpg.question: What is the difference between HEIC and JPEG?
jpg.answer: HEIC compresses photos about 50% better than JPEG at the same quality and supports extras like Live Photos and 16-bit color. JPEG's advantage is universal compatibility — every device, browser and app can open it. That's why converting HEIC to JPG is often necessary.

jpg.question: How do I convert HEIC to JPG on Windows or Mac?
jpg.answer: This converter works on both — nothing to install. If you'd rather use built-in tools, check our step-by-step guides for Windows and Mac linked in the footer below.

jpg.question: How do I convert HEIC to JPG on iPhone?
jpg.answer: Open this page in Safari on your iPhone and convert directly. To stop your iPhone from taking HEIC photos in the first place, go to Settings > Camera > Formats and select 'Most Compatible'.

png.question: How do I convert HEIC to PNG?
png.answer: Drop your HEIC file into the converter above and press Convert. The PNG file downloads instantly — no upload, no signup, and it works in any modern browser.

png.question: Is HEIC to PNG conversion lossless?
png.answer: Yes. PNG uses lossless compression, so every pixel of the decoded HEIC image is preserved exactly. It's the best choice when maximum quality matters.

png.question: Does PNG keep transparency from HEIC?
png.answer: Yes. If your HEIC image contains an alpha channel (transparency), converting to PNG preserves it — unlike JPG, which flattens transparent areas.

png.question: Why is my PNG file bigger than the original HEIC?
png.answer: HEIC uses very efficient lossy compression while PNG is lossless, so PNG files are typically 3-10x larger. That's the trade-off for perfect quality and universal compatibility.

png.question: Is this HEIC to PNG converter free?
png.answer: Completely free — no watermarks, no registration, no limits on the number of files you convert.

pdf.question: How do I convert HEIC to PDF?
pdf.answer: Select one or more HEIC files above and press Convert. You'll get a single PDF document containing your photos — created entirely in your browser, with no upload.

pdf.question: Can I combine multiple HEIC photos into one PDF?
pdf.answer: Yes. This converter accepts multiple HEIC files at once and merges them into a single multi-page PDF — handy for receipts, documents and photo collections.

pdf.question: Can I turn scanned HEIC photos into a PDF?
pdf.answer: Yes. If you photographed documents with your iPhone, just drop those HEIC files here and they'll be combined into a clean PDF you can share or archive.

pdf.question: Is the HEIC to PDF converter free?
pdf.answer: Yes — free, unlimited and private. Files never leave your device, so even sensitive documents stay safe.

pdf.question: Will my photo quality be preserved in the PDF?
pdf.answer: Yes. Images are embedded at high quality, and you can adjust the quality slider before converting to balance sharpness against file size.

batch.question: How do I batch convert HEIC to JPG?
batch.answer: Select all your HEIC files at once (Ctrl/Cmd-click or drag a whole selection), choose JPG as the output format and press Convert. Every file is converted in your browser and bundled into a ZIP.

batch.question: How many HEIC files can I convert at once?
batch.answer: There's no fixed limit — hundreds of files work fine. Since conversion runs on your own device, very large batches simply take a bit longer.

batch.question: Can I bulk convert HEIC to JPG for free?
batch.answer: Yes. Bulk conversion is completely free with no file limits, no watermarks and no registration — unlike most desktop bulk converters.

batch.question: How do I download all the converted files?
batch.answer: When the batch finishes, press 'Download ZIP' and you'll get a single ZIP archive containing every converted image.

batch.question: Can I batch convert HEIC to PNG or WebP too?
batch.answer: Yes. Before converting, choose PNG (lossless) or WebP (smallest files) as the output format instead of JPG.

viewer.question: How do I open a HEIC file without installing anything?
viewer.answer: Drag your HEIC files onto this page. The viewer decodes them in your browser and shows them instantly — no codecs, no apps, no account. It works on Windows 10 and 11, Mac, Linux, and Chromebooks.

viewer.question: Are my photos uploaded to a server?
viewer.answer: No. Everything happens locally in your browser using JavaScript. Your photos never leave your device, which makes this viewer safe for private pictures.

viewer.question: Can I view multiple HEIC files at once?
viewer.answer: Yes — select or drop as many files as you want and they'll appear in a gallery. Click any photo to see it full size in a new tab.

viewer.question: Can I save the photos as JPG?
viewer.answer: Yes. Every photo in the gallery has a JPG download button. For whole folders, the batch converter downloads everything as a single ZIP.

viewer.question: Why does Windows say it needs a codec to open HEIC?
viewer.answer: Windows needs the HEIF Image Extensions (and often the paid HEVC codec) to decode HEIC natively. This viewer skips that entirely by decoding the image in your browser.

home.question: What does OpenMyHEIC do?
home.answer: OpenMyHEIC is a free set of browser tools for HEIC photos taken on iPhone and iPad. You can convert HEIC to JPG, PNG, PDF, WebP, GIF or BMP, convert dozens of files at once with the batch tool, or simply view HEIC files without converting them. Everything runs locally in your browser.

home.question: Which output format should I choose?
home.answer: For sharing photos and maximum compatibility, choose JPG. For screenshots, graphics or when you need lossless quality, choose PNG. To combine several photos into one document, choose PDF. For publishing on the web with smaller file sizes, choose WebP. GIF and BMP cover legacy software that accepts nothing else.

home.question: Do I need to install anything or create an account?
home.answer: No. OpenMyHEIC works entirely in your web browser — there is nothing to download or install, no account, no signup and no watermarks. It works on Windows, Mac, Linux, Chromebook, Android and iPhone.

home.question: How is OpenMyHEIC different from other HEIC converters?
home.answer: Most online converters upload your photos to their servers, which is slow and raises privacy questions. OpenMyHEIC decodes and converts your photos on your own device using in-browser code, so your files never leave your computer. That also makes it faster for large batches — there is no upload or download wait.

home.question: Are my photos really private?
home.answer: Yes. The conversion happens with JavaScript and WebAssembly inside your browser tab. Your photos are never transmitted to us or to any third party — you can even load the page, disconnect from the internet, and the converter keeps working.

home.question: Is there a limit on how many photos I can convert?
home.answer: There is no artificial limit and no paywall. The practical limit is your device's memory: on a typical laptop you can comfortably convert hundreds of photos in one session using the batch converter.

gif.question: Why would I convert HEIC to GIF?
gif.answer: GIF is accepted almost everywhere, including old forums, legacy software and messaging tools that reject HEIC. It is a good choice for simple graphics or when a platform explicitly requires GIF. For normal photos, JPG or PNG will look better.

gif.question: Will my photo lose quality as a GIF?
gif.answer: Photos usually will. GIF is limited to a palette of 256 colors, so smooth gradients like skies and skin tones can show visible banding. Screenshots, drawings and graphics with few colors convert to GIF with no noticeable loss.

gif.question: Can this create an animated GIF from a Live Photo?
gif.answer: No. A HEIC file from a Live Photo contains a still image plus a short video, and this tool converts the still frame only. To turn a Live Photo into an animation, export it as a video on your iPhone first and use a video-to-GIF tool.

gif.question: Will the GIF be smaller than my HEIC file?
gif.answer: Not necessarily. For photographs, a GIF is often larger than the original HEIC and larger than an equivalent JPG, because GIF compression was not designed for photos. Use GIF for compatibility, not to save space.

gif.question: When should I use PNG instead of GIF?
gif.answer: Almost always, unless the receiving software specifically demands GIF. PNG supports millions of colors and full transparency, produces better quality, and is just as widely supported in modern applications.

bmp.question: Why would anyone still need BMP files?
bmp.answer: BMP survives in niches: old Windows programs, embroidery and engraving machines, label printers, industrial and medical software, and some game modding tools accept only BMP. If a program rejects everything modern, an uncompressed BMP usually works.

bmp.question: Is BMP conversion lossless?
bmp.answer: Yes in the sense that the decoded pixels are stored exactly as they are, with no additional compression artifacts. Note that HEIC itself is a lossy format, so the BMP preserves the decoded image perfectly but cannot recover detail the iPhone already compressed away.

bmp.question: How big will my BMP files be?
bmp.answer: Big. BMP stores every pixel uncompressed, roughly 3 bytes per pixel: a 12-megapixel iPhone photo becomes a BMP of around 36 MB, compared to 1–2 MB as HEIC. Convert to BMP only when the target software requires it.

bmp.question: Do Windows and Mac open BMP natively?
bmp.answer: Yes. BMP has been supported by every version of Windows since the early 1990s, and macOS Preview opens it too. That universality is exactly why some legacy workflows still standardize on it.

bmp.question: Is there a better alternative to BMP?
bmp.answer: If your software accepts it, PNG gives you the same lossless quality at a fraction of the file size. Use our HEIC to PNG converter unless BMP is explicitly required.

webp.question: Why convert HEIC to WebP?
webp.answer: WebP is the best format for publishing photos on the web: at the same visual quality it produces files roughly 25–35% smaller than JPG, which makes pages load faster. All modern browsers support it, and CMS platforms like WordPress accept WebP uploads natively.

webp.question: WebP or JPG — which should I choose?
webp.answer: Choose WebP when the destination is a website, blog or online store you control. Choose JPG when you are emailing photos, printing, or sending files to people whose software you do not know — JPG remains the safest universal option.

webp.question: Will the conversion lose quality?
webp.answer: The converter re-encodes your photo, which is technically lossy, but at the quality level used the difference is not visible in normal viewing. You keep the full resolution of the original HEIC.

webp.question: What can't open WebP files?
webp.answer: Very old browsers (Internet Explorer), Photoshop versions before 23.0 without a plugin, and a few older email clients and photo viewers. If your audience may use legacy software, JPG is the safer choice.

webp.question: Does WebP support transparency like PNG?
webp.answer: Yes, WebP supports transparency — but iPhone photos have no transparent areas, so for HEIC conversion this rarely matters. It becomes relevant if you later edit the image and need an alpha channel.

privacy.metaDescription: Learn how OpenMyHEIC protects your privacy. All file conversions happen in your browser — we never upload, store, or access your photos.
privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.introText: At OpenMyHEIC.com, we are committed to protecting your privacy. This Privacy Policy explains how we handle information when you use our HEIC file conversion service. Our core principle is simple: your files never leave your device.
privacy.dataCollectionText: OpenMyHEIC is designed to process files entirely in your browser. We do NOT collect, upload, store, or have access to any files you convert. We may collect anonymous usage data such as page views, browser type, and general geographic region through standard web analytics tools to improve our service. No personally identifiable information is collected through the conversion process.
privacy.cookiesText: We use essential cookies to ensure the proper functioning of our website, such as remembering your language preference. Third-party services like Google AdSense may use cookies to serve personalized ads based on your browsing history. You can manage cookie preferences through your browser settings. By using our website, you consent to the use of cookies as described in this policy.
privacy.thirdPartyText: We use Google AdSense to display advertisements on our website. Google AdSense may use cookies and web beacons to collect information about your visits to this and other websites in order to provide relevant advertisements. Google's use of advertising cookies enables it and its partners to serve ads based on your visit to our site and/or other sites on the Internet. You may opt out of personalized advertising by visiting Google's Ads Settings (https://adssettings.google.com).
privacy.userRightsText: Since we do not collect personal data through our conversion service, there is typically no personal data for us to manage on your behalf. However, if you believe we hold any personal information about you, you have the right to: request access to your data, request correction of inaccurate data, request deletion of your data, and object to processing of your data. To exercise any of these rights, please contact us at contact@openmyheic.com.
privacy.changesText: We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. Any changes will be posted on this page with an updated revision date. We encourage you to review this policy periodically to stay informed about how we protect your information.
privacy.contactText: If you have any questions about this Privacy Policy, please contact us at contact@openmyheic.com.

viewer.h1: HEIC Viewer — Open and View HEIC Files Online
viewer.intro: Can't open your iPhone photos? Drop your HEIC files below and see them instantly — no software to install, nothing uploaded.
viewer.dropTitle: Drop your HEIC files here to view them
viewer.aboutTitle: Why can't I open HEIC files?
viewer.aboutText1: HEIC is the photo format iPhones use by default since iOS 11. It saves storage, but Windows 10, many Android devices, older software, and most websites can't display it without extra codecs. That's why a photo that looks fine on your iPhone shows an error on your PC.
viewer.aboutText2: This HEIC viewer decodes your photos directly in your browser using JavaScript — your files never leave your device. It works on any system with a modern browser: Windows, Mac, Linux, Chromebooks. View your photos, check them full size, and download the ones you need as JPG — or convert entire folders with the batch converter.

sections.h2: Convert HEIC to JPG right in your browser
p: Drop your iPhone photos into the box above and this HEIC to JPG converter does the rest: your browser decodes each HEIC file locally and re-encodes it as a standard JPG you can download in seconds. Nothing ever leaves your device. There is no upload step, no server processing your pictures, no account to create and no watermark on the result. That matters more than it sounds — photos of your family, your documents or your whiteboard notes should not have to sit on a stranger's server just to change file format.

The reason this conversion exists at all is a compatibility gap. Since iOS 11 (2017), iPhones and iPads shoot HEIC by default because it packs the same picture into roughly half the space of a JPEG. But the wider world has not caught up: Windows 10 machines without extra codecs, older Android phones, many company intranets, government upload forms, print kiosks and plenty of email clients still choke on HEIC. JPG, by contrast, has been the universal language of photos since the 1990s — every device, browser, app and website opens it. Converting HEIC to JPG gives you a copy that works everywhere while your originals stay exactly as they were.

Because everything runs in the browser, the same page works on Windows, macOS, Linux, ChromeOS, Android and even on the iPhone itself. Convert a single photo before attaching it to an email, or change a whole batch of vacation shots in one pass — the tool processes files in parallel and hands you individual downloads or a single ZIP.

sections.h2: Ways to convert HEIC to JPG compared
p: This tool is not the only way to change HEIC to JPG — Apple, Microsoft and various websites all offer a route. Each comes with trade-offs in privacy, speed and cost, and the honest answer is that the best method depends on how many photos you have and where they are. Here is how the realistic options stack up.

table.caption: Ways to convert HEIC to JPG compared
rows: Fast — drag in dozens at once, download as ZIP
Converting existing photos on any device without installing anything
Not a converter — only changes the format of photos you take from now on
Stopping new HEIC files at the source; does nothing for photos you already have
Slow — open and re-save photos one at a time
HEIF extension is free, but the required HEVC Video Extensions cost $0.99 on Windows 10
Occasionally opening HEIC files on a PC you control
Decent — Preview can export a multi-selection in one go
Mac owners converting folders they have already copied to the computer
Photos are uploaded to a third-party server
Depends on your upload speed and their queue; large batches are slow
Often free with limits, paid tiers for bulk
Nothing this tool doesn't already do — and you give up privacy to get it

pAfter: The pattern worth noticing: every method that keeps your photos on your own hardware is either single-platform, manual, or only affects future photos. A browser-based converter is the one option that combines local processing with batch speed on every operating system — which is exactly why this page exists.

sections.h2: JPG vs other output formats: which should you choose?
p: JPG is the default output here for a reason — it is the format the rest of the world expects — but it is not always the right answer. This site can also turn HEIC into PNG, PDF and WebP, and each has a legitimate use case. The sizes below are rough figures for a typical 12 MP iPhone photo; your results will vary with the content of the image.

table.caption: JPG vs other output formats for a 12 MP photo
rows: Sharing, uploading, email, social media — the safe default for photos
Lossy, but visually indistinguishable at high quality settings
Screenshots, graphics, images you plan to edit repeatedly
Sending photos as a document: receipts, IDs, forms, multi-page scans
Same as the image embedded inside it
Similar to JPG, plus document overhead
Publishing images on your own website
Lossy or lossless; better compression than JPG

pAfter: If you are unsure, pick JPG. It opens on a 2005 digital photo frame and on next year's flagship phone alike. Reach for PNG only when you truly need lossless (and can stomach files ten times larger), PDF when the recipient asked for “a document”, and WebP when you control the platform where the image will live.

sections.h2: Troubleshooting: when HEIC to JPG conversion misbehaves
p: Browser-based conversion is reliable, but a few situations trip people up. Almost all of them have simple explanations — and none of them mean your photos are damaged.
list: Large batches stall or the tab crashes. Decoding HEIC is memory-hungry, and every photo in flight lives in your browser's RAM. If you feed it 300 photos on a laptop with many tabs open, the browser may run out of memory. Close unused tabs and convert in batches of 50–100 — it takes barely longer and never falls over.

A Live Photo converts to a still image. That is expected. A Live Photo is really a HEIC still plus a short video clip stored alongside it; the JPG keeps the key frame and the motion is left behind. If you want the moving part, export the Live Photo as a video on your iPhone instead.

A .heic file from a Samsung phone or Google Photos won't convert cleanly. Some Android “motion photos” are containers with a video stream embedded inside the image file. Most convert fine to a still JPG, but if one fails, open it in your phone's gallery app, save it as a still image, and convert that.

The JPG is bigger than the HEIC it came from. Completely normal, and not a sign that anything went wrong. HEIC uses HEVC compression, which is far more efficient than JPEG's 1992-era algorithm — that efficiency is the whole reason Apple adopted it. Re-encoding to JPG trades file size for compatibility; expect the JPG to be roughly 1.5–2× the size at comparable quality.

A file simply refuses to convert. Occasionally a file with a .heic extension is not actually HEIC — renamed files and partial downloads are the usual culprits. Re-export the photo from its source and try again.

sections.h2: When NOT to convert HEIC to JPG
p: An honest converter should also tell you when to leave your photos alone. If your photos live entirely inside the Apple ecosystem — iPhone, iPad, Mac, iCloud, AirDrop between them — there is no reason to convert anything. Every Apple device since 2017 handles HEIC natively, and Apple's own sharing features quietly convert to JPEG on the fly when they detect an incompatible recipient.

Think twice before bulk-converting an entire photo library, too. Converting does not replace your HEIC files; it creates JPG copies. Do that to a 50 GB library and you now store both versions — and because JPGs run larger than their HEIC sources, you have more than doubled the space those photos occupy.

There is also a quality dimension conversion cannot preserve. Portrait-mode HEIC files carry a depth map that lets you re-edit the background blur later; modern iPhone photos embed an HDR gain map that makes highlights pop on compatible screens. Both are stripped in the flat JPG copy. The picture still looks good — it just loses those editable, display-enhancing extras.

The sensible policy: keep your HEIC originals as the archive, and convert to JPG on demand — for the specific photos you need to email, upload or hand to someone outside the Apple bubble. Best of both worlds: maximum quality and minimum storage at home, maximum compatibility on the way out.

sections.h2: A workflow that scales: from one photo to a whole folder
p: For a one-off conversion, drag a single file in and you are done in a couple of seconds. For bigger jobs, select everything at once — you can drop an entire folder's worth of HEIC files in a single gesture, and the converter queues them automatically. Filenames are preserved, with only the extension changing from .heic to .jpg, so your photos stay matched to their originals and sort in the same order.

Converted files land wherever your browser saves downloads — typically the Downloads folder on a computer, or the Files app on an iPhone. When you convert many photos at once, grabbing the ZIP is the tidy option: one download, unzip, done. And if you find yourself doing this every week, consider fixing it upstream on your iPhone once, then keeping this page bookmarked for the backlog of HEIC photos you already have.

sections.h2: HEIC to PNG: when lossless is the right call
p: Use the converter above to change HEIC to PNG directly in your browser — free, private, and with no upload, since every conversion runs locally on your device. PNG uses lossless compression, which means the decoded image is stored pixel for pixel with nothing thrown away. That makes HEIC to PNG the right choice whenever quality outranks file size: photos you plan to edit heavily, screenshots with sharp text, diagrams, logos, scanned documents, and images headed for high-quality print.

The clearest case for PNG is repeated editing. Every time you save a lossy format like JPG, the encoder re-compresses the image and discards a little more detail — fine textures blur and blocky artifacts creep in around edges. A PNG can be opened, edited, and re-saved as many times as you like with zero added degradation, because nothing is ever thrown away. If a photo is going through several rounds of cropping, color correction, or retouching before its final export, convert HEIC to PNG once at the start and do all the work from that master copy.

PNG is also the safest bet for compatibility. It has been a web and software standard since the 1990s, so it opens everywhere — old Windows machines, corporate upload forms, government portals, email clients, and every image editor ever made. When a website rejects your iPhone photo or a colleague says they cannot open it, a PNG version ends the conversation.

sections.h2: The transparency myth: what alpha actually means for iPhone photos
p: PNG is famous for transparency, and that reputation causes a common misunderstanding. Yes, PNG supports an alpha channel — pixels can be fully or partially see-through, which is why logos and interface graphics are usually PNGs. But a photo taken with an iPhone camera has no transparency to begin with: every pixel in the frame is a fully opaque piece of the scene. Converting that HEIC to PNG does not magically add a transparent background; you get the exact same rectangular photo, just stored losslessly.

So when does the alpha channel actually matter? After the conversion, in your editing workflow. If you plan to cut a subject out of the background in Photoshop, GIMP, Canva, or a background-removal tool, you need a format that can hold the transparent result — and PNG is that format. Starting from a PNG also means the edges you cut will be clean, because there are no JPG compression artifacts smearing the boundary between subject and background. In short: convert HEIC to PNG not because the photo is transparent, but because you are about to make part of it transparent.

sections.h2: File size reality check: what a 12 MP iPhone photo really weighs
p: The number-one surprise when people convert HEIC to PNG is the file size. It is worth seeing the honest numbers side by side for a typical 12-megapixel iPhone photo:

table.caption: File size reality check (typical 12 MP iPhone photo)
rows: Modern lossy compression (HEVC-based), extremely efficient — that's exactly why Apple uses it
Older lossy compression; roughly twice the size of HEIC at comparable visual quality
Lossless — every pixel stored exactly, and photographic detail compresses poorly without loss
Essentially uncompressed; 12 million pixels at 3 bytes each, no compression at all

pAfter: A PNG being 10x larger than the HEIC it came from is normal and expected — it is the honest cost of keeping every pixel. That cost is worth paying for a photo you are about to edit or archive at maximum quality. It is not worth paying for a photo you just want to text to a friend. For everyday sharing, JPG delivers a file most people cannot tell apart from the PNG at a fraction of the weight, which is why this site offers both conversions.

sections.h2: Workflows where converting HEIC to PNG wins
list: Design tools: Figma, Canva, and presentation apps handle PNG flawlessly, and any cutouts, overlays, or exports you make will keep clean, artifact-free edges.

OCR and documents: text recognition works noticeably better on lossless images — JPG artifacts around letterforms are exactly the kind of noise that trips up OCR engines, so receipts, whiteboards, and scanned pages should go through PNG.

Screenshots and UI graphics: flat colors, sharp lines, and small text are the worst case for lossy compression and the best case for PNG, which compresses those areas efficiently and keeps them razor sharp.

Multi-step editing: when a photo passes through several tools — crop in one app, color-correct in another, annotate in a third — using PNG as the intermediate format means only the final export involves any quality loss at all.

Archiving a master copy: if you are keeping one definitive version of an important photo before making derivative versions, a lossless PNG master guarantees future edits always start from full quality.
pAfter: The common thread: PNG belongs in the middle of a workflow, wherever the image will be processed again. It preserves maximum information for the next step, whether that step is an editor, an OCR engine, or you in five years.

sections.h2: When NOT to use PNG
p: PNG is the wrong choice more often than people expect, and the reason is always the same: weight. Sending PNGs by email is a fast way to hit attachment limits — three or four converted iPhone photos can exceed a typical 25 MB cap on their own. Messaging apps like WhatsApp will accept the file but re-compress it aggressively on delivery, destroying the lossless quality you paid all that file size for. For anything you are sending to another person, convert HEIC to JPG instead.

The same logic applies at scale. Converting an entire vacation album to PNG turns a 2 GB photo library into 20 GB of files that look identical on every phone and laptop screen. For bulk conversion, use JPG — or simply leave the photos in HEIC if they are staying on Apple devices, where the format works natively. And if the destination is a website you run, neither PNG nor JPG is ideal for photographs: WebP delivers JPG-level sizes at better quality, and this site's HEIC to WebP converter covers that case. Reserve PNG on the web for graphics, logos, and screenshots — the content it was designed for.

sections.h2: Troubleshooting HEIC to PNG conversions
p: My PNG is enormous. Night-mode shots, landscapes, and any photo full of fine detail produce the largest PNGs, because lossless compression cannot summarize complex textures the way lossy formats do — a detailed 48 MP photo from a recent iPhone can produce a PNG well over 50 MB. This is normal behavior, not an error. If the size is a problem, the photo probably wanted to be a JPG.

A large batch is slowing my browser down. Each PNG is built in your browser's memory before it downloads — that is the flip side of the no-upload privacy model. Dozens of photos means hundreds of megabytes of pixel data held in RAM at once. If a big batch feels sluggish or a tab runs out of memory, convert in smaller groups of 10–20 files and let each group finish before starting the next.

Will PNG improve my photo's quality? No — and any tool that claims otherwise is misleading you. HEIC is a lossy format, so some detail was discarded the moment the photo was saved. Converting to PNG preserves everything that survived that first compression perfectly, but it cannot reconstruct detail that no longer exists. Think of PNG as a perfect photocopy: identical to what you gave it, never better.

sections.h2: Why convert HEIC to PDF? Turning iPhone photos into real documents
p: Most of the time, the photos on your iPhone are just photos. But sometimes a picture is really a document in disguise: a receipt you snapped for an expense report, a signed contract you photographed on the kitchen table, a page of homework, a whiteboard covered in meeting notes. When you convert HEIC to PDF, you take that photo out of the "image" world and put it into the "document" world — the format that every office, school, government portal and accounting department already expects.

PDF has three practical advantages over sending a raw photo. It is universally accepted: any phone, computer or printer opens a PDF the same way, with no compatibility questions. It holds multiple pages in a fixed order, so a set of related photos travels as one tidy file instead of a loose pile of attachments. And it prints with predictable layout — page size and margins are baked into the file, so what you see is what comes out of the printer.

This free HEIC to PDF converter does all of that directly in your browser. Your photos are decoded and assembled into the PDF locally on your own device, and nothing is uploaded to a server — which matters when the photo in question is an ID, a medical form or a signed agreement.

sections.h2: Common uses: receipts, contracts, homework and multi-page PDFs
p: The single most common reason people turn an iPhone photo into a PDF is the camera-as-scanner workflow. You photograph a paper document, and now you need to email it somewhere it will be taken seriously. Attaching a sideways HEIC file to a formal email looks improvised — and on Windows it may not even open. Convert it to PDF first and the same photo arrives as a proper document: right way up, one page, double-clickable everywhere.

The second big use case is combining several HEIC photos into one multi-page PDF. Drop in all the pages of a contract, every receipt from a business trip, or the ten photos of a school project, and you get back a single file with the pages in the order you arranged them. One attachment is easier to send, easier to file, and impossible to deliver incomplete — nobody can open page 3 without also having pages 1 and 2. Design portfolios, expense claims and homework submissions all benefit from the same trick.

Finally, there are the platforms that simply refuse anything else. University application systems, visa and immigration portals, insurance claims and many government websites accept uploads only as PDF. If the evidence they want exists as HEIC photos on your iPhone, converting them to PDF is the only way through the upload form.

sections.h2: PDF vs JPG vs PNG: which format for sharing documents?
p: Choosing between PDF and an image format comes down to a simple question: is the recipient supposed to read this like a document, or use it like a picture? This table sums up how the three formats behave when you share something document-like:

rows: Yes — many photos, one file, fixed order
Yes — page size and margins are part of the file
Yes — the default for offices, schools and portals
Limited — needs a PDF editor to change pages
Depends on the app and printer settings
Sometimes — often treated as "just a photo"
Yes — opens in any image editor
Depends on the app and printer settings
Rarely — seen as a screenshot or graphic
Yes — opens in any image editor

pAfter: The pattern is clear: PDF wins whenever the photos belong together and need to be treated formally, while JPG and PNG win whenever the recipient needs to crop, retouch or reuse the image itself. That is why this site offers both — convert HEIC to PDF for paperwork, and HEIC to JPG or PNG for everything visual.

sections.h2: Quality, page size and printing: what to expect
p: A PDF page has a fixed size — typically the proportions of a sheet of paper — while your iPhone photo has its own resolution and aspect ratio. When the converter places a HEIC photo on a PDF page, the image is scaled to fit the page while keeping its proportions, and the page orientation follows the photo: portrait photos produce portrait pages, landscape photos produce landscape pages. Nothing is cropped and nothing is stretched.

Print quality depends on the resolution of the original photo, not on the PDF wrapper. A 12-megapixel iPhone photo has far more pixels than a printed letter-size page can show, so document photos taken with any recent iPhone print crisply. If the source photo is a small thumbnail or a heavily compressed screenshot, putting it inside a PDF will not add detail that was never there.

File size follows the same logic. A PDF made from photos is essentially a thin container around compressed images, so it weighs roughly what the equivalent JPGs would, plus a little overhead per page. A ten-photo PDF of full-resolution iPhone shots can easily reach 15–20 MB — worth knowing before you email it, as covered in the troubleshooting section below.

sections.h2: When NOT to convert HEIC to PDF
p: PDF is the right destination for paperwork, but it is the wrong one for plenty of everyday situations. Skip the PDF conversion when:
list: The recipient needs to edit or crop the image. Pulling a photo back out of a PDF is clumsy; send a JPG or PNG and they can open it straight in any editor.

The photo is headed for social media, a chat app or a website. Those platforms want image files — use HEIC to JPG for photos or WebP for web pages, where they display inline instead of behind a document icon.

You care about archival quality. A PDF built from photos stores compressed versions of them. To preserve the best copy of an image, keep the original HEIC (or export a lossless PNG) and treat the PDF as the shareable version, not the master.
You are sending a single casual photo to another person. A lone snapshot wrapped in a PDF adds a download-and-open step for no benefit — an image attachment previews instantly.
pAfter: A good rule of thumb: convert HEIC to PDF when the file will be filed, printed or submitted; stay with an image format when the file will be viewed, edited or posted.

sections.h2: Troubleshooting HEIC to PDF conversions
p: One big PDF or one PDF per photo? If your photos are pages of the same document, merge them into a single multi-page PDF — that is what recipients expect from a contract or a report. If they are unrelated items, such as separate receipts for different expense lines, convert them in smaller groups or one at a time so each PDF stands on its own. For very large batches, converting in chunks of a few dozen photos also keeps your browser fast, since all the work happens locally in memory.

PDF looks blurry when you zoom in? That is the resolution ceiling of the original photo, not a conversion defect. A PDF can only show the pixels the HEIC contained, so zooming far beyond 100% will soften any photo-based PDF. For documents, the fix is at capture time: fill the frame with the page, hold the phone parallel to it, and make sure the text is sharp in the photo before converting.

Attachment too large to email? Most providers cap attachments around 20–25 MB, and a multi-page PDF of full-resolution photos can hit that limit quickly. The lightest fix is to lower the image quality before converting — document photos stay perfectly readable at reduced quality. Alternatively, convert to JPG first at lower quality, or split the document into two smaller PDFs. If a portal rejects your upload, check its stated size limit; trimming quality is usually all it takes.

sections.h2: HEIC and WebP: two modern formats, two different worlds
p: HEIC and WebP are closer relatives than most people realize. Both are modern, highly efficient image formats born from video technology — HEIC compresses photos with HEVC, the codec behind 4K video, while WebP grew out of the VP8 codec used in online streaming. Both exist to store the same picture in far less space than the JPG standard from the 1990s. The difference is where each one lives. HEIC is the native format of the Apple ecosystem — what your iPhone writes to its camera roll and what iCloud syncs between devices. WebP is the native format of the web — what browsers, CDNs and site speed tools are optimized to serve.

That split is exactly why you would convert HEIC to WebP. Your iPhone photos are already efficiently compressed, but in a dialect the web does not speak natively. Many people convert HEIC to JPG first and then run the JPG through a separate WebP optimizer; going straight from HEIC to WebP skips the middle step, avoids a second generation of compression loss, and produces a publish-ready file in one pass — entirely in your browser, with nothing uploaded anywhere.

sections.h2: From iPhone camera roll to a fast-loading website
p: The most common reason to convert heic to webp is publishing: you shot product photos, blog images or portfolio shots on an iPhone, and now they need to go onto a site you run. The workflow is short. Export or AirDrop the HEIC files from your photo library to your computer, drop them into the converter above, and download WebP files that WordPress (natively supported since version 5.8), Shopify and virtually every modern site builder will accept as a normal image upload. No plugin, no server-side conversion step, no format errors at upload time.

The payoff shows up in your Core Web Vitals. Largest Contentful Paint — how quickly the biggest element on the page renders — is usually decided by a hero image, and a hero served as WebP arrives noticeably faster than the same image as JPG simply because there are fewer bytes to download. The effect compounds on image-heavy pages: a product grid or thumbnail gallery saves that margin on every file, often the difference between a page that feels instant on mobile data and one that visibly pops in as it loads. Since page experience feeds into search rankings, converting iPhone photos to WebP before upload is one of the cheapest SEO wins available.

One practical habit: resize before you serve. A 12 MP iPhone photo is around 4000 pixels wide, but a blog column rarely displays more than 1200–1600. Convert to WebP at full resolution here, keep that as your upload master, and let your CMS generate the display sizes — WordPress and Shopify both do this automatically for WebP uploads, so thumbnails and hero crops inherit the smaller format.

sections.h2: Same 12 MP photo, three destinations
p: Format choice is really a question about where the photo is going. Here is how one typical 12 MP iPhone photo behaves in the three formats this site produces most often:

rows: Fastest — smallest download, best LCP scores
Websites, blogs, online stores, anywhere page speed is measured
Fine for sharing, heavier to serve at scale
Email attachments, printing, recipients with unknown software
Slowest — sizes are impractical for photo pages
Design handoff, screenshots, graphics that must stay pixel-perfect

pAfter: The numbers vary with the scene — a busy landscape compresses differently than a product shot on white — but the ranking almost never changes. For anything served over the network to visitors, WebP wins. For anything sent to a person rather than a page, JPG is the safer courier. PNG is a specialist tool, not a photo format.

sections.h2: WebP compatibility in 2026: where it works, where it still doesn't
p: On the web itself, compatibility stopped being a question years ago: every current browser on every platform renders WebP, and has since Safari completed the set in 2020. Publishing platforms followed: WordPress accepts WebP uploads natively since 5.8, Shopify serves it automatically, and modern site builders and forum software treat it as a first-class image format. If your file's final destination is a URL, you can convert heic to webp with confidence.

The rough edges are all off the web. Older Photoshop releases (before version 23) cannot open WebP without a plugin, some bundled Windows desktop photo viewers still stumble on it, and email clients are inconsistent — a WebP attached to an email may render inline for one recipient and arrive as an unopenable mystery file for another. None of this is a flaw in your converted file; it is simply software that predates the format's mainstream adoption.

The decision rule that follows is simple: match the format to the destination, not the source. Photo headed for a page you publish? WebP. Photo headed for a person, a printer or an inbox? Convert that copy to JPG instead — and there is nothing wrong with producing both from the same HEIC.

sections.h2: When not to use WebP
p: WebP is the right answer for the web, but it is the wrong answer often enough that a short checklist is worth keeping:
list: Email attachments — inline rendering support across email clients is still unpredictable; send JPG so every recipient can open the photo without friction.
Print jobs — commercial print shops and home photo printers are built around JPG, TIFF and PDF workflows; handing a print service WebP files invites a conversion step you do not control.
Client deliverables — when you do not know what software the recipient uses, JPG removes the possibility of a 'this file won't open' reply.
Your master archive — keep the original HEIC (or a PNG export for edited work) as the source of truth; WebP is a distribution format, generated from the master when needed, never the only copy.
Images you will keep re-editing — each save of a lossy WebP re-compresses the pixels; do the editing on the original and export WebP last.
pAfter: The pattern in all five cases is the same: WebP is optimized for serving, not for exchanging or preserving. Use it at the end of the pipeline, not the beginning.

sections.h2: Two things that look like errors but aren't
p: If a platform rejects your .webp upload, resist the temptation to rename the file to .jpg — the extension is just a label, and the receiving software will still find WebP data inside and fail, sometimes with a more confusing error than before. The real fix is to convert the HEIC to an actual JPG and upload that; a rejected WebP simply means that service has not added support yet.

The second surprise: occasionally a converted WebP comes out larger than the HEIC it came from. This is normal, not a malfunction. HEVC — the compression inside HEIC — is an exceptionally strong codec, and on some images it simply out-compresses WebP. The comparison that matters for the web is not WebP versus the HEIC original, which browsers cannot display anyway, but WebP versus the JPG you would otherwise have served — and there WebP reliably comes out ahead. An unusually large conversion changes nothing about the workflow: the file still renders everywhere and still does its job on the page.

sections.h2: Convert HEIC to GIF: An Honest Look at a 1987 Format
p: GIF is one of the oldest image formats still in daily use. It was released in 1987, back when computers displayed a few hundred colors and a 100 KB file was considered heavy. Nearly four decades later, GIF survives not because it produces beautiful images, but because virtually every piece of software ever written can open one. That universal compatibility is the real reason to convert HEIC to GIF: your iPhone saves photos in a modern, efficient format that plenty of older systems simply refuse to read, while a GIF sails through everywhere.

This converter turns your HEIC files into GIF images directly in your browser. Nothing is uploaded to a server, there is no queue and no account, and your photos never leave your device. Drop the files above, download the results, done. Before you do, though, it is worth understanding what GIF does to a photograph, because for most everyday pictures another format will serve you better. The sections below explain exactly when a HEIC to GIF conversion is the right call, and when it is not.

sections.h2: Where GIF Still Genuinely Wins
p: There is a real, if narrow, set of situations where GIF is not just acceptable but the correct answer. They almost all come down to one thing: the receiving system was built in an era when GIF was the standard, and it never moved on.
list: Forums and legacy web software that only accept GIF uploads. Some long-running bulletin boards, intranet tools and enterprise systems still have upload filters written decades ago that reject anything else.
Avatars and icons on older platforms. Certain community sites, game launchers and chat systems from the 2000s still expect small GIF avatars and will not process a HEIC at all.
Simple graphics and low-color screenshots. A logo, a diagram or a screenshot of a plain interface uses few colors, which is exactly what GIF's palette was designed for. These convert cleanly.
Embedded systems, kiosks and signage players. Industrial displays, point-of-sale terminals and older digital-signage hardware often ship with decoders that understand GIF, JPG and nothing newer.
pAfter: If your situation matches one of these, converting HEIC to GIF here takes seconds and solves the problem completely. If it does not, keep reading before you commit.

sections.h2: What Happens to Different Content Types as GIF
p: Not every image suffers equally when it becomes a GIF. The format's fixed color palette punishes some content hard and leaves other content untouched. Here is what to realistically expect from the most common things people convert:

rows: Visible banding in skies, skin and shadows
Convert only if GIF is required
Text stays sharp; solid areas convert cleanly
Heavy banding and blotchy noise in dark areas
Worst case for GIF; avoid if possible

pAfter: The pattern is simple: the fewer distinct colors and smooth gradients an image contains, the better it survives the trip to GIF. Detailed photographs sit at the wrong end of that spectrum; graphics and clean screenshots sit at the right one.

sections.h2: What to Expect Technically from the Conversion
p: When you convert HEIC to GIF, three things happen to the image data, and knowing them ahead of time avoids surprises. First, the color space collapses. A HEIC photo can contain millions of distinct colors; a GIF stores at most 256. The converter chooses the best possible palette for your specific image and then applies dithering, a technique that scatters pixels of the available colors to simulate shades that no longer exist. Dithering makes gradients look smoother from a distance, at the cost of a faint grainy texture up close.

Second, the output is a single static frame. If your HEIC came from a Live Photo, only the key still image is converted; the motion component is not carried over, so the resulting GIF does not animate. Third, there is no transparency to preserve. Photos never contain transparent regions, so the alpha channel that GIF technically supports simply does not come into play here. What you get is a flat, fully opaque, palette-based image, faithful to the original within the hard limits of a 256-color format.

sections.h2: Better Options in Most Cases
p: Here is the honest advice: if nothing on the receiving end forces your hand, GIF is rarely the best destination for an iPhone photo. For ordinary photographs, converting HEIC to JPG gives you full color, small files and compatibility with effectively everything made in the last twenty-five years. For screenshots, documents and graphics where you want pixel-perfect sharpness, HEIC to PNG is the stronger choice, with millions of colors and lossless quality. And if the image is destined for your own website, WebP delivers smaller files than either at comparable quality, though a handful of older tools still cannot open it.

Stay with GIF when the requirement is explicit: an upload form that rejects everything else, a legacy system you cannot change, or a workflow that has standardized on GIF and is not worth fighting. In those cases the compatibility is the whole point, and the quality trade-off is simply the price of admission. Every format mentioned here is available on this site, so switching your target is one click away.

sections.h2: Troubleshooting: Big Files and Rough Colors
p: Two outcomes surprise people the most, and neither is a malfunction. The first is a GIF that is dramatically larger than the HEIC it came from. A detailed 12-megapixel photo can balloon from a 2 MB HEIC into a GIF several times that size, because GIF's compression scheme was designed for flat areas of repeated color, not for photographic detail. The conversion is working exactly as designed; the format is just a poor fit for that content. If the size matters, that photo wants to be a JPG.

The second is dirty-looking color in smooth areas: a sunset that shows stripes, a wall that looks speckled, skin that appears slightly gritty. That is the 256-color ceiling and the dithering that compensates for it, both baked into the GIF specification itself. No converter, online or offline, can produce a GIF of a gradient-rich photo without some of this. When you see it, the image has hit the limit of what the format can represent, and the fix is choosing a format without that limit.

sections.h2: The Format That Opens Everywhere: Why BMP Still Matters
p: BMP is the closest thing digital imaging has to a lowest common denominator. The format is so simple — a short header followed by a plain grid of pixel values — that there is almost nothing to decode, which is why virtually every program written in the last three decades can read it, no matter how old or obscure. When you convert HEIC to BMP, you are translating Apple's newest and most tightly compressed photo format into the oldest and most universally understood one still in everyday use.

That simplicity is exactly why this page exists. Almost nobody should store their photo library as BMP in 2026, and we would never recommend it for sharing or archiving. But a steady trickle of specialized software — much of it written long before HEIC existed and never updated since — still refuses anything else. If a stubborn program rejects your iPhone photos no matter which format you feed it, an uncompressed bitmap is very often the file that finally goes through. This converter produces that file in your browser, without your photo ever leaving your device.

sections.h2: Software and Devices That Commonly Require BMP
p: The demand for HEIC to BMP conversion is concentrated in a handful of niches where equipment or software was designed around simple, predictable pixel data. These are the situations we see most often:

rows: Digitizing tools that turn images into stitch patterns often predate modern formats and parse only basic bitmap data
Crop tightly to the design before converting; bold, high-contrast images digitize far better than busy photos
Toolpaths are generated directly from a raw pixel grid, so many controllers accept only uncompressed input
Keep the resolution close to what the machine can physically engrave; convert to grayscale inside the engraving software afterward
Thermal printer firmware is tiny and typically reads only the simplest BMP headers
These printers are low-resolution by design — downscale the image dramatically before sending it, or the print will be illegible
They were written when BMP was the one safe assumption on every Windows PC
If the program also lists JPG support, try JPG first; it will load faster and use far less memory
Older game engines stored textures as uncompressed bitmaps, and the modding utilities built around them kept that requirement
Check the mod documentation for required dimensions — many engines expect power-of-two sizes such as 512 × 512
Certified systems are rarely updated after approval, so their supported formats freeze at the time of certification
Follow the vendor's file specification exactly, and test with a single image before converting a whole batch

pAfter: These are general patterns rather than guarantees — individual machines vary, so the manual for your specific device is always the final word. But if your equipment falls into one of these categories and rejects modern formats, converting HEIC to BMP is the standard first fix.

sections.h2: The Simple Math Behind Giant BMP Files
p: There is no mystery to BMP file sizes: with no compression at all, every pixel costs about 3 bytes — one each for red, green, and blue. Multiply that by the pixel count and you know the file size before you convert. A 12-megapixel photo works out to roughly 36 MB. A shot from a 48-megapixel iPhone main camera balloons to around 144 MB — a single photo several times larger than most email attachment limits.

Scale that up and the practical consequences are immediate. Ten 48-megapixel conversions approach 1.5 GB of disk space, a batch of a hundred would not fit on many USB sticks, and uploading them to cloud storage takes as long as uploading a short video. This is why BMP is a delivery format for one specific machine, never a storage format for your library. The compressed HEIC originals hold the same picture in a fraction of the space.

sections.h2: A Smarter Workflow: Convert Only What the Machine Needs
p: Because each converted file is so large, the efficient approach is surgical rather than wholesale:
list: Select only the specific photos your machine or program actually needs for the job at hand — never convert an entire album to BMP.
Convert them here, feed the BMP files to the software or device, and confirm the job worked.
Keep the original HEIC files as your master copies. They are the highest-quality, smallest version of each photo you own.
Delete the BMP copies once the job is done. You can regenerate them from the originals in seconds whenever you need them again.
If the target software also accepts PNG, use PNG instead — same lossless pixels, a fraction of the size.
pAfter: Treating BMP as a temporary, disposable export keeps your storage under control while still satisfying whatever legacy tool insisted on it.

sections.h2: Troubleshooting: When the Legacy Program Still Rejects Your BMP
p: Occasionally a converted file gets refused even though the software claims BMP support. The most common cause is color depth. This converter writes standard 24-bit BMPs — the modern default that nearly everything reads — but some very old or very specialized programs expect 8-bit indexed color or even 1-bit black and white. If that's your situation, open the 24-bit BMP in a basic editor such as Microsoft Paint and re-save it at the depth the program asks for; the heavy lifting of getting out of HEIC is already done.

The second common failure is sheer size. Software from the era when BMP ruled was written for images a few hundred pixels wide, and a 48-megapixel bitmap can make it freeze, crash, or silently refuse the file. The fix is to reduce the resolution before converting — crop or resize the photo on your iPhone, or export a smaller copy from any editor — so the resulting bitmap has dimensions the old program can actually handle. A smaller BMP also transfers faster to printers and engraving machines, which often have limited onboard memory of their own.

sections.h2: When you just need to see the photos
p: It usually starts with a batch of photos someone shared from an iPhone — by email, a messaging app, or a USB cable — and a folder full of files ending in .heic that your computer refuses to display. You don't want to install codecs, create an account, or convert files you haven't even seen yet. You just want to look at the pictures.

That's exactly what this HEIC viewer is for. Drop the files onto the page and they appear as a gallery in seconds, decoded directly in your browser. Nothing gets installed, nothing gets uploaded, and you don't have to convert a single photo unless you decide to. If a picture turns out to be worth keeping, a one-click button saves it as a JPG.

sections.h2: Why HEIC won't open, by platform
p: If you've ever wondered why the same photo opens fine on one device and fails on another, the answer is codec support. HEIC images are compressed with HEVC, a codec that not every operating system licenses or ships by default. Here's how the major platforms handle it — and the fastest way around each gap.

rows: No, not out of the box
Needs the HEIF Image Extensions plus the HEVC Video Extensions from the Microsoft Store — and the HEVC one costs $0.99
View the files here in your browser, no codecs required
HEIF support is included, but the HEVC codec most iPhone photos rely on is sometimes missing
Open the photos in this viewer, then convert only the ones you need
Only versions older than macOS 10.13 predate the format
Update macOS, or view HEIC online from any browser
Support varies by manufacturer and Android version, and many gallery apps still stumble on it
Open the files in this viewer from any mobile browser
ChromeOS ships without a built-in HEIC decoder
This browser-based viewer works on Chromebooks as-is
Most distributions don't include HEIF libraries by default
Skip the package hunt and open the files right here

pAfter: The pattern is easy to spot: native support is inconsistent everywhere outside recent Apple systems. A browser-based HEIC viewer sidesteps the entire problem, because the decoding happens inside the web page itself rather than in your operating system.

sections.h2: View or convert? Sometimes looking is enough
p: Not every HEIC file needs to become a JPG. Converting is a means to an end, and if the end is simply knowing what's in the photos, viewing them is faster. Opening HEIC files in a viewer is the right call when you want to:
list: Check what someone actually sent you before doing anything else with it
Skim a large set of photos and pick out the handful worth keeping or converting
Verify that one specific shot — a document, a whiteboard, a receipt — is sharp and readable
Confirm that files copied from a phone or memory card transferred correctly
pAfter: Conversion earns its place the moment you need to do something with a photo: edit it in a program that doesn't understand HEIC, attach it where a JPG is expected, upload it to a website, or send it for printing. For those cases, download individual photos as JPG straight from the gallery, or send the whole set to the batch converter and get everything back as JPG, PNG, or PDF in one pass.

sections.h2: How the viewer opens HEIC files in your browser
p: There's no server doing the work behind the scenes. When you drop a HEIC file onto this page, your browser reads it and decodes it locally using JavaScript and WebAssembly — a technology that lets a web page run near-native code. The decoded image is drawn on screen straight from your device's own memory.

That design has two practical consequences. First, it behaves identically on every operating system: Windows 10 without codecs, a locked-down work laptop, a Chromebook, a Linux desktop — if it runs a modern browser, it can view HEIC online. Second, your photos never travel anywhere. There's no upload step, no processing queue, and no copy of your images sitting on someone else's server, which matters when the pictures are private.
Saving as JPG works the same way: the browser re-encodes the decoded image on your device and hands you the file. Even that step never touches the network.

sections.h2: Where those mystery .heic files come from
p: A handful of everyday situations account for most "HEIC won't open" moments:
list: Email attachments: iPhones often attach original photos as HEIC, especially when files are sent from the Files or Photos app instead of pasted into the message body
AirDrop and messaging apps: AirDrop transfers originals by default, and some apps pass files through untouched — so the HEIC arrives exactly as it was shot
SD cards and cable transfers: reading an iPhone's storage or a memory card on a PC copies the raw .heic files, bypassing the automatic conversion iOS applies in many sharing flows
iCloud downloads on Windows: pulling your photo library down to a PC can leave you with folders of HEIC files that most desktop programs can't preview
pAfter: In all of these cases the files themselves are perfectly fine — the receiving computer just lacks a decoder. Opening them in this viewer confirms in seconds that nothing was lost along the way.

sections.h2: Troubleshooting: when a HEIC file still won't display
p: Occasionally a file resists even a browser-based viewer. Three causes cover almost every case:
list: It isn't really a HEIC file. A JPG or PNG that was renamed with a .heic extension keeps its original structure inside, and a HEIC decoder can't parse it. If you suspect a rename, change the extension back — or run the file through the converter, which inspects the actual contents

It's a burst or Live Photo container. HEIC can hold several images, or a still plus a short video clip, in a single file. The viewer displays the main key frame; the motion part of a Live Photo lives in a separate video track that a still-image viewer doesn't play

The file is incomplete. An interrupted transfer — a dropped connection, a cable pulled too soon, a full disk — can leave a truncated file that no viewer can fully decode. Copy the original again from the source device and retry
pAfter: A quick sanity check: if a photo opens fine on the iPhone that took it but fails everywhere else, the transfer is the prime suspect. Send it again over a reliable connection, and avoid services that compress or repackage attachments along the way.

sections.h2: Batch convert HEIC files in bulk — no uploads, no waiting
p: There are moments when converting photos one at a time simply isn't an option: you're emptying an entire iPhone camera roll before switching to Android, backing up three years of family pictures to a Windows PC, or preparing hundreds of shots from a wedding or vacation to share with people whose devices can't open HEIC. This batch HEIC converter is built for exactly that job. Add dozens or hundreds of HEIC files at once, choose JPG, PNG or WebP, and download everything together as a single ZIP archive.

The reason a browser-based batch converter can outrun a cloud service comes down to one word: upload. A cloud converter has to receive your files before it can touch them, and a camera roll of 500 HEIC photos is easily 1-2 GB — on a typical home connection that upload alone can take longer than the entire conversion does here. Because this tool converts multiple HEIC files directly on your own device, the upload step disappears entirely. It also means your photos never leave your computer, there is no server-imposed file limit, and the tool works just as well on a slow or metered connection.

sections.h2: How to work through a large batch efficiently
p: Bulk HEIC to JPG conversion is straightforward, but a few habits make big jobs noticeably smoother — especially once you get past a couple hundred files:
list: Select everything in one go: open the folder with your exported photos, press Ctrl+A (Cmd+A on Mac), and drag the whole selection into the converter instead of picking files one by one.
Work in rounds of 50-100 files on an average computer. Several medium rounds finish more reliably than one giant one, and you can check results as you go.
Keep the converter tab in the foreground while a batch runs. Browsers throttle background tabs to save power, which can slow the job dramatically.
Close memory-hungry apps and spare browser tabs first — video calls, games and editors compete for the same RAM the conversion needs.
Use the ZIP download rather than saving files individually. One archive is faster to save, easier to move, and avoids a barrage of download prompts.
Spot-check a handful of converted images before deleting anything, so you know the quality setting is where you want it.

sections.h2: How many photos can you convert at once?
p: Because everything runs locally, the practical batch size depends on your device's memory and processor rather than on any rule of the site. These are honest, conservative orders of magnitude for typical 12-megapixel iPhone photos:

rows: Split a camera roll into two or three rounds and download a ZIP after each.
Close other tabs and apps first; one big round is usually fine.
Full camera-roll exports are realistic in just a few large rounds.
Keep the screen awake and plugged in; small rounds are much more reliable.

pAfter: Resolution matters as much as file count: 48-megapixel photos from a recent iPhone use several times the memory of standard shots, so halve these numbers if your library is full of them. If a batch feels sluggish, don't push toward the ceiling — a smaller round that completes cleanly always beats a huge one that stalls.

sections.h2: What to expect while a big batch runs
p: Once you start a batch, each file is decoded and re-encoded in sequence, and the progress indicator ticks forward as every photo completes — so you can always see how far along the job is rather than staring at a spinner. On a modern laptop, expect roughly one to three seconds per photo; older machines and high-resolution images take longer, and a phone can need several seconds per file. A 200-photo batch on a mid-range laptop, then, is typically a five-to-ten-minute job you can leave running while you do something else on another window.

Two behaviors are normal and worth knowing about. First, the browser may pause briefly between files or between groups of files — that's the page yielding time back to the system so the tab stays responsive, not a crash. Second, if an individual photo fails to convert (a corrupted download or a video clip mislabeled as an image, for example), it is flagged and skipped while the rest of the batch continues. Nothing fails silently, and one bad file never costs you the other hundred and ninety-nine.

sections.h2: Switching from iPhone to Android or Windows? Convert everything once
p: The single most common reason to bulk convert HEIC to JPG is a platform move. A typical migration looks like this: export your photos from the camera roll or download the originals from iCloud onto a computer, drag the entire set into the batch converter, convert everything to JPG, and then import the ZIP's contents into Google Photos, your Android phone's storage, or a Pictures folder on Windows. From that point on, every photo opens everywhere — old Windows versions, smart TVs, car displays, photo kiosks and web upload forms included.

One piece of advice before you delete anything: keep the original HEIC files as your master copies if you have the space. HEIC stores the same image in roughly half the size of an equivalent JPG, which makes the originals the cheaper archive — and converting is a one-way trip, since a JPG re-encoded from HEIC can't recover any detail lost along the way. A sensible setup is HEIC originals in a backup folder or external drive, converted JPGs in circulation for daily use.

sections.h2: Troubleshooting large batches
p: If the tab freezes or crashes on a very large batch — say you dropped 1,000 files in at once — the fix is almost always to divide the job. Reload the page, add half the files, and let that round finish before starting the next; on machines with less memory, quarters work better than halves. Nothing is lost when a tab stalls, because your original files are untouched on disk.

If only the first file downloads and the rest never appear, your browser is blocking multiple automatic downloads — look for a prompt near the address bar and allow downloads from this site, or simply use the ZIP option, which sidesteps the issue entirely by delivering everything as one file. And if your camera roll contains duplicate names (every iPhone eventually produces more than one IMG_0001.HEIC), converted files are renamed with automatic suffixes so nothing overwrites anything else inside the ZIP.
